name: Prisma Raw Query SQL Injection
description: Developer using Prisma's $queryRaw or $executeRaw with string interpolation instead of tagged template literals allows attackers who control input values to inject arbitrary SQL, reading or modifying any data in the database.
mitigations: Use $queryRaw and $executeRaw exclusively with tagged template literals, never string concatenation
Prefer Prisma's type-safe query API (findMany, update, etc.) over raw queries
Add a linter rule to flag $queryRaw usage in code review
Run automated SQL injection tests (sqlmap, custom payloads) in CI

name: SQLAlchemy Mass Assignment via Model Update
description: API endpoint passing unfiltered request body fields directly to SQLAlchemy's model update allows attackers to overwrite columns they should not control (e.g., is_admin, account_balance, user_id).
mitigations: Allowlist permitted fields before passing to ORM update — never use **request.json directly
Use separate input schemas (Pydantic/Marshmallow) to whitelist writable attributes
Add integration tests asserting that privileged fields (is_admin) cannot be set via the API
Log and alert on unexpected field write attempts

name: Mongoose NoSQL Operator Injection
description: Express application passing raw JSON body fields directly into Mongoose query conditions allows attackers to inject MongoDB query operators ($gt, $where, $regex) to bypass authentication or extract arbitrary records.
mitigations: Sanitize all query inputs to remove MongoDB operator keys before querying
Use express-mongo-sanitize middleware to strip $ and . prefixes from request bodies
Define strict Mongoose schemas with type validation
Avoid constructing queries from user-controlled objects — build queries explicitly

name: Drizzle Template Literal SQL Injection
description: Developer using Drizzle ORM's sql template tag with JavaScript string interpolation instead of placeholder syntax introduces SQL injection vulnerabilities when user input is embedded directly in the query.
mitigations: Use Drizzle's typed query builder (db.select, db.insert) instead of raw sql template strings
When sql`` is necessary, use placeholder parameters: sql`WHERE id = ${userId}`
Audit all sql`` occurrences in the codebase for interpolated user values
Enable automated SAST rules targeting template literal SQL construction

name: ORM N+1 Query DoS via Unprotected Endpoint
description: API endpoint triggering ORM lazy-loading relationships without pagination or depth limits allows attackers to craft requests that generate thousands of database queries, exhausting connection pools and starving legitimate traffic.
mitigations: Use eager loading (include/joinedload) for known relation traversals
Enforce query depth limits and maximum page sizes on all list endpoints
Implement connection pool limits and query timeouts
Use DataLoader pattern or batch loading to cap query count per request

name: Prisma Schema Migration File Leakage
description: Prisma migration files committed to a public repository or accessible via a misconfigured file server expose the complete database schema, column names, and data types, enabling attackers to craft targeted injection or enumeration attacks.
mitigations: Keep the prisma/ directory out of public repository access
Add prisma/migrations to .gitignore for public-facing repositories
Restrict access to migration files in CI/CD artifacts
Treat schema information as sensitive — include it in threat models

name: Mongoose Prototype Pollution via Untrusted Object Merge
description: Application using lodash.merge or Object.assign with unvalidated request body before passing to Mongoose corrupts JavaScript prototype chain, potentially overriding security checks or injecting malicious properties into all objects.
mitigations: Validate and whitelist input fields before any object merge operation
Use JSON Schema or Mongoose schema validation as the first line of defense
Replace lodash.merge with safe alternatives that reject prototype keys
Keep Node.js and Mongoose updated to versions with prototype pollution patches

name: ORM LIKE Query Wildcard for User Enumeration
description: Search endpoint passing user-supplied search terms directly into an ORM LIKE query without wildcard escaping allows attackers to use crafted patterns to enumerate email addresses, usernames, or other sensitive fields efficiently.
mitigations: Escape LIKE wildcards (%, _) in user-supplied search terms before querying
Rate-limit and authenticate search endpoints
Restrict searchable fields to non-sensitive columns
Implement minimum search term length requirements

name: SQLAlchemy Async Session Shared Across Requests
description: FastAPI or Starlette application reusing a single SQLAlchemy async session across concurrent requests causes session state cross-contamination, where one user's pending uncommitted writes are visible to another user's query within the same transaction.
mitigations: Create a new SQLAlchemy session per request using dependency injection
Use contextlib.asynccontextmanager to scope sessions to individual request lifetimes
Never store session objects as module-level singletons
Add integration tests asserting session isolation between concurrent requests

name: ORM Bulk Update Without Ownership Verification
description: API endpoint using ORM bulk update (updateMany/update_all) filtered only by attacker-controllable ID list without verifying record ownership allows horizontal privilege escalation — one user modifying another user's records.
mitigations: Always include user_id = current_user.id in update conditions alongside any user-supplied IDs
Use row-level ownership checks in ORM queries, not only in application logic
Write tests that assert cross-user update is rejected with 403
Log bulk update operations with requesting user identity

name: Mongoose Missing Nested Object Validation
description: Mongoose schema with shallow validation allows attackers to submit deeply nested objects in request payloads that bypass top-level validation, writing unexpected nested fields to documents or triggering application-level errors.
mitigations: Define strict Mongoose schemas with strict: true to reject extra fields
Validate nested objects with embedded schemas, not plain Object types
Use express-validator or Joi for request-level validation before touching the ORM
Enable Mongoose's runValidators option on update operations

name: Drizzle Type Coercion Bypass in Filter Conditions
description: Drizzle ORM application implicitly coercing query parameters from string to integer allows attackers to supply type-confusion payloads (e.g., '0 OR 1=1') that bypass expected filter conditions when the ORM applies loose JavaScript type comparison.
mitigations: Parse and validate all query parameters to their expected types before passing to Drizzle
Use Zod or similar for route parameter schemas with strict type enforcement
Avoid implicit type coercion in filter values — always use explicit parseInt/parseFloat
Write boundary tests for filter conditions with type-confusion payloads
